steps.title: Looking Ahead — Future Time Words
steps.desc:
Unit 7 gave you past time words. Unit 8 gave you conditionals.

Now: the future.

Before learning the future tense grammar, you need the vocabulary to place events in the future.
goals: Review and expand the time word system

steps.exampleEn: What are you going to do tomorrow?
steps.note:
내일: tomorrow.

You learned this in Unit 7.
내 (來: coming) + 일 (日: day).

내일 뭐 할 거예요?
= Tomorrow what will-you-do?
You'll learn 할 거예요 in Lesson 2.

steps.exampleEn: There's an exam next week.
steps.note:
다음: next/following.

다음 주: next week
다음 달: next month
다음에: next time

이번 주: this week
이번 달: this month
이번에: this time

steps.exampleEn: What are you doing this weekend?
steps.note:
이번: this (current).
주말: weekend (주 + 말: week + end).

이번 주말 = this weekend.

이번 주말에 뭐 해요?
= One of the most common Korean social questions.

You can answer with:
집에서 쉴 거예요. (I'm going to rest at home.)
친구를 만날 거예요. (I'm going to meet a friend.)

steps.exampleEn: I'll call you later.
steps.note:
나중에: later, sometime later.

나중: later time.
에: time particle.

나중에 봐요! = See you later!
나중에 이야기해요. = Let's talk later.

Non-specific future — could be hours or days.

steps.exampleEn: I'll arrive soon.
steps.note:
곧: soon.

곧 가요: I'm going soon.
곧 시작해요: It starts soon.
곧 도착해요: I'll arrive soon.

도착하다: to arrive.
도착 (到着): 도 (arrive) + 착 (reach).

steps.exampleEn: I want to go to Korea someday.
steps.note:
언젠가: someday, at some point.

언제 (when) + ㄴ가 (indefinite particle).

언젠가 한국에 가고 싶어요.
= Someday I want to go to Korea.

Far future, unspecified time.

뭔가: something (뭐 + ㄴ가)
어딘가: somewhere (어디 + ㄴ가)
누군가: someone (누구 + ㄴ가)

steps.title: 📆 Complete Time Word System
steps.text:
PAST: 어제 / 지난주 / 지난달 / 작년
PRESENT: 오늘 / 이번 주 / 이번 달 / 올해
FUTURE: 내일 / 다음 주 / 다음 달 / 내년

SPECIFIC → VAGUE:
내일 (tomorrow) — specific
다음 주 (next week) — near future
곧 (soon) — vague near future
나중에 (later) — vague
언젠가 (someday) — distant/unspecified

All go at the START of the sentence:
내일 학교에 가요. ✓
학교에 내일 가요. △ (possible but less natural)

steps.hint: 나중에 = later. 나중에 봐요 = see you later.
곧 = soon. 곧 도착해요 = arriving soon.
언제 = when. + ㄴ가 (indefinite) = someday/sometime.

steps.q: 이번 주말에 뭐 해요? 이번 means:
steps.hint: 이번 = this (current cycle). 이번 주말 = this weekend.
도 (到) = arrive. 착 (着) = reach. 도착 = arrival.

steps.s: ___ 전화할게요. (I'll call you later.)
steps.hint: 나중에 = later.

steps.s: ___ 한국에 가고 싶어요. (I want to go to Korea someday.)
steps.hint: 언젠가 = someday.

steps.s: ___ 주말에 뭐 할 거예요? (What are you doing this weekend?)
steps.hint: 이번 = this (current). 이번 주말 = this weekend.

steps.s: {1} 뭐 할 거예요? {2} 친구를 만날 거예요. {3} 한국에 가고 싶어요.
steps.hint: What will you do tomorrow? I'll meet a friend next week. I want to go to Korea someday.

steps.q: Which time word goes FURTHEST into the future?
steps.hint: 언젠가 = someday (distant, unspecified future).

steps.title: The Future Tense — Your Second Tense Car
steps.desc:
Remember the Train?
Stem = engine. Past tense car = 았/었/했.

Now: the FUTURE tense car.

-(으)ㄹ 거예요 = will / going to.

This is the most common way to express future in Korean.
goals: Apply to vowel and consonant stems

steps.exampleEn: I'm going to go to school tomorrow.
steps.note:
-(으)ㄹ 거예요: will / going to.

The Train: Stem + [future car: -(으)ㄹ 거] + 예요

Formation:
Vowel stem + ㄹ 거예요: 가다 → 갈 거예요
Consonant stem + 을 거예요: 먹다 → 먹을 거예요
하다 → 할 거예요

것 (thing) + 이다 (to be) = 거예요 (colloquial).

갈 거예요 = 'the thing of going exists' = will go.

steps.exampleEn: I'm going to go to school tomorrow.
steps.note:
가다: vowel stem 가.
가 + ㄹ 거예요 = 갈 거예요.

하다 → 할 거예요 (will do)
오다 → 올 거예요 (will come)
보다 → 볼 거예요 (will see/watch)
마시다 → 마실 거예요 (will drink)

steps.exampleEn: I'm going to eat samgyeopsal for dinner.
steps.note:
먹다: consonant stem 먹.
먹 + 을 거예요 = 먹을 거예요.

있다 → 있을 거예요 (will be/have)
없다 → 없을 거예요 (won't be/have)
읽다 → 읽을 거예요 (will read)

steps.exampleEn: I'm going to live in Seoul.
steps.note:
살다: ㄹ stem.

ㄹ stems already end in ㄹ, so just add 거예요:
살다 → 살 거예요 (not 살을 거예요)
알다 → 알 거예요 (will know)
만들다 → 만들 거예요 (will make)
놀다 → 놀 거예요 (will play/hang out)

steps.title: 🔮 Future Tense Formation
steps.text:
The Train now has TWO tense cars:

PAST: stem + 았/었/했 + 어요
먹 + 었어요 = 먹었어요 (ate)

FUTURE: stem + (으)ㄹ 거예요
먹 + 을 거예요 = 먹을 거예요 (will eat)

Formation rules:
Vowel stem → +ㄹ 거예요: 갈, 올, 볼, 할
Consonant stem → +을 거예요: 먹을, 있을, 읽을
ㄹ stem → +거예요: 살, 알, 만들

Negative future:
안 + future: 안 갈 거예요 (won't go)
못 + future: 못 갈 거예요 (can't go — future)

Question:
뭐 할 거예요? = What will you do?

steps.exampleEn: I will eat well. (said before eating)
steps.note:
-겠습니다: formal future (합쇼체).

You've heard this:
잘 먹겠습니다: I will eat well (before meal — gratitude)
잘 하겠습니다: I will do well (commitment)
알겠습니다: I understand / understood (formal)

-겠- is the formal future/intention marker.
-겠 + 습니다 = formal polite future.

⚡ JRTF: You don't need to produce this yet.
Just recognize it. Full 합쇼체 production in Lesson 4.

steps.hint: Consonant stem + 을 거예요. 먹을 거예요.
Vowel stem + ㄹ 거예요. 가 + ㄹ = 갈 거예요.
하다: 하 + ㄹ = 할 거예요.
ㄹ stem: already ends in ㄹ → just add 거예요.
잘 먹겠습니다 = I will eat well = thank you for the food (before eating).

steps.q: 내일 뭐 ___ 거예요? (What will you do tomorrow?)
steps.hint: 하다 → 할 거예요. Question: 뭐 할 거예요?

steps.s: 내일 영화를 ___ 거예요. (I'm going to watch a movie tomorrow.)
steps.hint: 보다 → 볼 거예요. 보 + ㄹ = 볼.

steps.s: 이번 주말에 친구를 ___ 거예요. (I'm going to meet a friend this weekend.)
steps.hint: 만나다 → 만날 거예요. 만나 + ㄹ = 만날.

steps.s: 한국어를 열심히 ___ 거예요. (I'm going to study Korean hard.)
steps.hint: 공부하다 → 공부할 거예요.

steps.s: 내일 학교에 {1} 거예요. 한국어를 {2} 거예요. 저녁에 친구를 {3} 거예요.
steps.hint: Tomorrow I'll go to school. I'll study Korean. In the evening I'll meet a friend.

steps.q: The Train now has ___ tense cars:
steps.hint: Past: 았/었/했어요. Future: -(으)ㄹ 거예요. Present has no tense car — just stem + 아/어요.

steps.title: Because and Then — -아/어서 Deep Dive
steps.desc:
You used -아/어서 briefly in Unit 7.
Now: the full picture.

-아/어서 has TWO uses:
1. CAUSE: 배가 고파서 밥을 먹었어요. (Because I was hungry, I ate.)
2. SEQUENCE: 학교에 가서 공부해요. (I go to school and study.)

Plus one critical warning.

steps.exampleEn: Because I was hungry, I ate.
steps.note:
-아/어서 for CAUSE:

[reason] + -아/어서 + [result].

배가 고파서 밥을 먹었어요.
= Hungry → so → ate.

Formation (same vowel harmony as always):
Bright (ㅏ,ㅗ) → -아서: 가다 → 가서
Dark (others) → -어서: 먹다 → 먹어서
하다 → 해서

⚠️ Tense ONLY on the final verb:
배가 고파서 먹었어요. ✓
배가 고팠어서 먹었어요. ✗

steps.exampleEn: I go to school and (then) study.
steps.note:
-아/어서 for SEQUENCE:

[first action] + -아/어서 + [next action].

학교에 가서 공부해요.
= Go to school → and then → study.

일어나서 밥을 먹었어요.
= Got up → and then → ate.

Sequence implies natural flow:
First action leads naturally to the second.

steps.title: ⚠️ Critical Rule: No Imperatives After -아/어서
steps.text:
-아/어서 CANNOT be followed by commands or suggestions.

배가 고파서 밥 먹어요. ✓ (statement)
배가 고파서 밥 먹자! ✗ (suggestion — WRONG)
배가 고파서 밥 먹어! ✗ (command — WRONG)

For commands/suggestions, Korean uses -(으)니까:
배가 고프니까 밥 먹자! ✓

⚡ JRTF: -(으)니까 is formally taught in Unit 14.
For now: just know -아/어서 + command = WRONG.

This is one of the most common mistakes foreign learners make.
TOPIK tests this distinction heavily.

deepDive.title: Why This Rule Exists
deepDive.text:
-아/어서 presents cause as NEUTRAL FACT.
It doesn't carry the speaker's authority or justification.

-(으)니까 presents cause as the SPEAKER'S REASON.
It carries justification, so commands/suggestions can follow.

배가 고파서... = 'Given the fact that I'm hungry...'
→ Can state a result, but can't command someone.

배가 고프니까... = 'Since I'm hungry (and I'm telling you this)...'
→ Can justify a suggestion or command.

This distinction is taught fully in Unit 14.
For now: remember the restriction.

steps.exampleEn: (Having met you,) nice to meet you.
steps.note:
만나서 반갑습니다.

만나다 + 아서 = 만나서 (having met)
반갑다: glad/pleased.

Literally: 'Having met (you), I'm glad.'

This is one of the most common -아/어서 phrases.
You learned it in Unit 1 — now you know the grammar behind it!

steps.exampleEn: Thank you for helping.
steps.note:
도와줘서 고마워요.
= Because (you) helped, I'm thankful.

도와주다: to help (도와 + 주다: help + give).
도와줘서: because of helping.

-아/어서 + 감사합니다/고마워요 = 'thank you FOR [verb]-ing'.

와 줘서 감사합니다: thank you for coming.
기다려 줘서 고마워요: thanks for waiting.

steps.q: 배가 고파서 밥을 먹었어요. -아서 here means:
steps.hint: -아/어서 = because/so. Hungry → so → ate.
-아/어서 for sequence: go to school → and then → study.
-아/어서 + command/suggestion = WRONG. Use -(으)니까 instead.
만나서 = having met. 반갑다 = glad. → Nice to meet you.
-아/어서 = because. 'Because you helped, thank you.'

steps.s: 배가 고파___ 밥을 먹었어요. (Because I was hungry, I ate.)
steps.hint: -아/어서 = because. 고프다 → 고파 + 서.

steps.s: 늦어___ 미안해요. (Sorry for being late.)
steps.hint: 늦다 → 늦어서. Because (I was) late, I'm sorry.

steps.s: 학교에 가___ 공부했어요. (I went to school and studied.)
steps.hint: -아/어서 for sequence: went → and then → studied.

steps.s: 어제 늦게 일어나{1} 학교에 못 갔어요. 늦어{2} 선생님한테 미안하다고 했어요.
steps.hint: I woke up late so I couldn't go to school. I told the teacher I was sorry for being late.

steps.q: -아/어서 + imperative is wrong. Which connector allows imperatives?
steps.hint: -(으)니까 = speaker's justification → commands OK. JRTF for now.
와 줘서 = because (you) came (for me). 감사합니다 = thank you.

steps.exampleEn: On weekends I watch movies or exercise.
steps.note:
-거나: or (connecting verbs/adjectives).

Stem + 거나.
보다 → 보거나 (watch or...)
먹다 → 먹거나 (eat or...)
하다 → 하거나 (do or...)

주말에 영화를 보거나 운동해요.
= On weekends I watch movies or exercise.

아니면: or (between nouns): 커피 아니면 차?
-거나: or (between verbs): 보거나 먹거나.

steps.hint: -거나 = or (between verbs).

steps.title: The Three Speech Levels You Need
steps.desc:
You've been speaking 해요체 since Unit 1 Lesson 2.
You heard 합쇼체 named in Unit 3 Lesson 7b.
반말 was seeded: 'It exists. Don't use it yet.'

Now all three get their full treatment.

This is a P26 harvest. You already FEEL the difference.
Today you OWN it.
goals: Master 해요체 production (you already have this)
Know WHEN to use each level

steps.title: 🎭 You've Been Doing This Since Unit 1
steps.text:
Unit 1 Lesson 2: You learned -요 makes things polite.
Unit 3 Lesson 7b: 해요체 was formally named.
Every lesson since: you've been producing 해요체.

Unit 3 also showed you:
감사합니다, 안녕하십니까 — 합쇼체.
반말 exists but 'don't use it yet.'

Now: the full map.

Korean has 7 speech levels in grammar books.
In daily life, you need THREE:

해요체: polite casual (90% of your life)
합쇼체: polite formal (work, service, news)
반말: casual (close friends, younger people)

The other 4 are literary/archaic. Ignore them.

steps.exampleEn: I eat. I go. It's good.
steps.note:
해요체: the speech level you already know.

Present: 먹어요, 가요, 해요
Past: 먹었어요, 갔어요, 했어요
Future: 먹을 거예요, 갈 거예요, 할 거예요

Use with: everyone in daily life.
Street, cafe, friends' parents, colleagues, strangers.

해요체 = safe. When in doubt, use 해요체.

steps.exampleEn: Thank you. I will eat well.
steps.note:
합쇼체: the formal polite level.

Formation:
Vowel stem + -ㅂ니다: 가다 → 갑니다
Consonant stem + -습니다: 먹다 → 먹습니다

Past: -았/었 + 습니다: 갔습니다, 먹었습니다

Use with:
• News anchors, presentations
• Customer service: 감사합니다
• Military, formal speeches
• First meeting with someone much older
• Before meals: 잘 먹겠습니다

steps.exampleEn: Where are you going? (very formal)
steps.note:
Questions in 합쇼체:

Vowel stem + -ㅂ니까: 가다 → 갑니까?
Consonant stem + -습니까: 먹다 → 먹습니까?

어디 갑니까? = Where are you going? (very formal)
뭐 먹습니까? = What are you eating? (very formal)

You'll hear these on news and in very formal settings.
In daily life, 어디 가요? is normal.

steps.exampleEn: I eat. I go. It's good. (casual)
steps.note:
반말: casual speech. No -요.

해요체 → 반말: just drop -요.
먹어요 → 먹어
가요 → 가
좋아요 → 좋아
했어요 → 했어
갈 거예요 → 갈 거야

Use with:
• Close friends your age or younger
• Children
• Very close siblings
• NEVER with strangers, elders, or people you just met

⚠️ Using 반말 with the wrong person is a serious social mistake.

⚡ Full 반말 production practice: Unit 10 Lesson 3.

steps.title: 📋 When to Use Each Level
steps.text:
해요체 (폴라이트 캐주얼):
• Default for everything
• Friends you're not super close with
• Strangers
• Shopping, restaurants, cafes
• Parents of friends
• 90% of your Korean life

합쇼체 (폴라이트 포멀):
• Job interviews
• Presentations at work
• News broadcasts
• Customer service (from their side)
• Military
• Before/after meals: 잘 먹겠습니다 / 잘 먹었습니다
• First meeting with much older person

반말 (캐주얼):
• Close friends (same age or younger)
• Children
• Siblings (depending on family)
• Couples (after a while)
• NEVER to strangers or elders

The golden rule:
When in doubt → 해요체.
Better too polite than not polite enough.

steps.hint: Vowel stem + ㅂ니다. 가 + ㅂ니다 = 갑니다.
Consonant stem + 습니다. 먹 + 습니다 = 먹습니다.
반말 = drop -요. 먹어요 → 먹어.

steps.q: You're meeting your Korean friend's grandmother for the first time. Use:
steps.hint: First meeting with elder → 합쇼체 (most respectful).

steps.q: At a coffee shop ordering coffee:
steps.hint: Daily life with strangers → 해요체 (safe default).

steps.q: With your close friend who is your age:
steps.hint: Close friends same age → 반말 is natural and expected.

steps.s: 감사___. (Thank you — formal)
steps.hint: 감사하다 → 합쇼체: 감사합니다.

steps.s: 잘 먹___. (I will eat well — before meal, formal)
steps.hint: Before meals: 잘 먹겠습니다 (합쇼체 future).

steps.s: 해요체: 가{1}. 합쇼체: 갑{2}. 반말: {3}.
steps.hint: 해요체: 가요. 합쇼체: 갑니다. 반말: 가.

steps.q: Which speech level have you been using since Unit 1?
steps.hint: Every -아/어요 ending = 해요체. You've been using it from day one.

steps.title: Making Plans — Accept, Decline, Negotiate
steps.desc:
Korean social life runs on plans.

같이 밥 먹을까요? = Shall we eat together?
내일 시간 있어요? = Do you have time tomorrow?

This lesson practices making, accepting, and declining plans.
Plus a common excuse pattern you'll hear everywhere.

steps.exampleEn: Shall we watch a movie together?
steps.note:
-(으)ㄹ까요?: shall we? / should I?

Formation: same as future -(으)ㄹ + 까요?
Vowel stem + ㄹ까요: 갈까요? (shall we go?)
Consonant stem + 을까요: 먹을까요? (shall we eat?)
하다 → 할까요? (shall we do?)

Suggestion + question = -(으)ㄹ까요?

같이 갈까요?: shall we go together?
뭐 먹을까요?: what shall we eat?

steps.exampleEn: Do you have time tomorrow?
steps.note:
시간 있어요?: Do you have time?

시간 (時間): time.
시 (時: hour/time) + 간 (間: between/interval).

내일 시간 있어요?
= Do you have time tomorrow?

Answers:
네, 시간 있어요. (Yes, I have time.)
아니요, 시간 없어요. (No, I don't have time.)
미안해요, 바빠요. (Sorry, I'm busy.)

steps.exampleEn: Sounds good! Let's go together!
steps.note:
Accepting plans:

좋아요!: sounds good! / great!
그래요!: okay! / alright!
네, 좋아요!: yes, sounds good!
당연하죠!: of course! (당연하다: obvious + -죠: right?)

같이 가요!: let's go together!
같이 먹어요!: let's eat together!

steps.exampleEn: Sorry, I can't go tomorrow.
steps.note:
Declining plans:

미안해요, 못 가요: sorry, I can't go.
죄송해요, 약속이 있어요: sorry, I have plans.
다음에 갈게요: I'll go next time.

약속 (約束): promise / plans / appointment.

다음에: next time.
갈게요: I'll go (promise/intention — new ending!)
-(으)ㄹ게요: 'I'll do X' (promise to the listener).

steps.exampleEn: Why can't you come? — (You see,) I have plans.
steps.note:
약속이 있거든요.

-거든요: 'you see' / 'the reason is' / background explanation.

Why can't you come?
약속이 있거든요. = (You see,) I have plans.

-거든요 gives a REASON the listener didn't know.
It's like: 'the thing is...'

⚡ JRTF: -거든요 is formally named in Unit 13.
For now: just recognize it as 'the reason is...'

opts: Shall we watch a movie together?
I want to watch a movie
steps.ans: Shall we watch a movie together?
steps.hint: -(으)ㄹ까요? = shall we? 같이 = together.
약속 (約束) = promise/plans/appointment.
-거든요 gives a reason the listener didn't know.

steps.q: Someone invites you. You want to accept:
steps.hint: 좋아요 = sounds good! Accepting the invitation.

steps.q: Someone invites you. You must decline politely:
steps.hint: 미안해요 + reason = polite decline.

steps.s: 같이 밥 ___까요? (Shall we eat together?)
steps.hint: 먹다 → -(으)ㄹ까요? 먹을까요?

steps.s: 미안해요, 내일 ___ 가요. (Sorry, I can't go tomorrow.)
steps.hint: 못 = can't (inability/situation).

steps.s: 왜 못 와요? — 약속이 ___. (I have plans, you see.)
steps.hint: -거든요 = 'you see / the thing is...'

steps.s: A: 이번 주말에 같이 영화 {1}? B: 미안해요, {2}이 있거든요. A: 그럼 {3}에 봐요!
steps.hint: A: Shall we watch a movie this weekend? B: Sorry, I have plans. A: Then let's meet next time!
-(으)ㄹ게요 = 'I'll do X' (promise to you).

opts: ㄹ까요 is suggestion/question, ㄹ 거예요 is statement
ㄹ까요 is past, ㄹ 거예요 is future
ㄹ까요 is formal, ㄹ 거예요 is casual
steps.ans: ㄹ까요 is suggestion/question, ㄹ 거예요 is statement
steps.hint: 갈까요? = shall we go? 갈 거예요 = I will go.

steps.exampleEn: You speak like a Korean person.
steps.note:
처럼: like / as (comparison particle).

Noun + 처럼.
한국 사람처럼: like a Korean person.
아이처럼: like a child.
영화처럼: like a movie.

한국 사람처럼 말해요!
= You speak like a Korean!
(The best compliment for a Korean learner.)

steps.hint: 처럼 = like/as (comparison).

steps.title: Expanding -고 — Progressive and After
steps.desc:
You learned -고 (and/then) in Unit 7.

Now two important expansions:

-고 있다: progressive (is doing right now)
-고 나서: and after doing

One is a JRTF seed. One is new production.

steps.exampleEn: What are you doing right now?
steps.note:
-고 있다: is doing right now.

Stem + 고 있다.
먹고 있어요: is eating.
가고 있어요: is going.
공부하고 있어요: is studying.

지금 뭐 하고 있어요?
= What are you doing right now?

⚡ JRTF: This is formally named in Unit 11.
For now: recognize it.
지금 하고 있어요 = am doing right now.

steps.exampleEn: After eating, I drank coffee.
steps.note:
-고 나서: after doing.

Stem + 고 나서.
나다: to arise/come out. 나서 = after arising.

먹고 나서: after eating.
일어나고 나서: after waking up.
씻고 나서: after washing.

밥을 먹고 나서 커피를 마셨어요.
= After eating, I drank coffee.

-고 나서 emphasizes COMPLETION before the next action.
-고 (simple): and/then (looser connection).
-고 나서: and AFTER finishing (tighter sequence).

steps.exampleEn: After eating, I took a walk.
steps.note:
밥을 먹고 나서 산책했어요.

산책하다: to take a walk.
산책 (散策): stroll.
산 (散: scatter) + 책 (策: plan/walk).

-고 나서 is commonly used with daily routines:
일어나고 나서 씻어요. (After waking up, I wash.)
씻고 나서 밥을 먹어요. (After washing, I eat.)
밥을 먹고 나서 출근해요. (After eating, I go to work.)

steps.title: 📋 -고 Family Comparison
steps.text:
-고: and / and then (loose)
밥을 먹고 커피를 마셨어요.
= I ate and drank coffee. (two facts)

-고 나서: after doing (tight sequence)
밥을 먹고 나서 커피를 마셨어요.
= After eating, I drank coffee. (finish eating first)

-고 있다: progressive (right now) — JRTF
지금 밥을 먹고 있어요.
= I'm eating right now.

-고 싶다: want to (you already know this!)
밥을 먹고 싶어요.
= I want to eat.

opts: What are you doing right now?
What do you want to do?
steps.ans: What are you doing right now?
steps.hint: -고 있다 = progressive (is doing now).

steps.q: 밥을 먹고 나서 커피를 마셨어요. -고 나서 means:
steps.hint: -고 나서 = after doing/finishing.

opts: 고 나서 emphasizes completion before next action
steps.ans: 고 나서 emphasizes completion before next action
steps.hint: -고 = loose 'and.' -고 나서 = 'after finishing X, then Y.'
⚡ JRTF: Formally named in Unit 11.

steps.q: Which sentence means 'I'm studying right now'?
steps.hint: -고 있다 = is doing now. 공부하고 있어요 = am studying.

steps.s: 밥을 먹___ 나서 커피를 마셨어요. (After eating, I drank coffee.)
steps.hint: -고 나서 = after doing. 먹 + 고 나서.

steps.s: 지금 한국어를 공부___ 있어요. (I'm studying Korean right now.)
steps.hint: -고 있다 = progressive. 공부하 + 고 있어요.

steps.s: 씻___ 나서 밥을 먹어요. (After washing, I eat.)
steps.hint: 씻다 + 고 나서 = after washing.

steps.s: 지금 뭐 {1} 있어요? 밥을 {2} 있어요. 먹{3} 나서 공부할 거예요.
steps.hint: What are you doing now? I'm eating. After eating I'll study.
-고 나서 = after doing. Wake up → then → wash.
-고 있다 = is doing right now = present progressive.

steps.s: 지금 전화___ 있어요. 나중에 다시 전화할게요. (I'm on the phone right now. I'll call back later.)
steps.hint: -고 있다 = progressive. 전화하고 있어요 = on the phone right now.

steps.title: Two More Irregular Patterns
steps.desc:
You conquered ㄷ and ㅂ irregulars in Unit 7.

Now: ㄹ-irregular and 으-irregular.

These are the seeds planted in Unit 6 Lesson 5.
You've been using some of these without knowing the rule.
Now they get their names.
goals: Name the ㄹ-irregular pattern (ㄹ drops before certain consonants)
Name the 으-irregular pattern (ㅡ drops before vowel endings)
Apply both to present, past, and future
Know which common verbs belong to each

steps.en: ㄹ-irregular: ㄹ drops before ㄴ, ㅂ, ㅅ
steps.exampleEn: to know → knowing, know (formal), do you know?
steps.note:
THE RULE: When a ㄹ-stem meets ㄴ, ㅂ, or ㅅ, the ㄹ drops.

알다 (to know):
알 + ㄴ → 아는 (not 알는) — modifier
알 + ㅂ니다 → 압니다 (not 알ㅂ니다) — 합쇼체
알 + 세요 → 아세요 (not 알세요) — honorific

BUT ㄹ stays before other consonants:
알 + 고 = 알고 ✓ (ㅈ, ㄱ, ㄷ, etc. → ㄹ stays)
알 + 면 = 알면 ✓
알 + 거예요 = 알 거예요 ✓

steps.exampleEn: There are many people living in Seoul.
steps.note:
살다: ㄹ-irregular.

살 + ㄴ → 사는 (modifier: living)
살 + ㅂ니다 → 삽니다 (합쇼체)
살 + 세요 → 사세요 (honorific: please live/lives)

BUT:
살 + 고 = 살고 ✓
살 + 면 = 살면 ✓
살 + 았어요 = 살았어요 ✓
살 + 거예요 = 살 거예요 ✓

steps.en: to make / to open / to sell
steps.exampleEn: Please open the door.
steps.note:
More ㄹ-irregulars:

만들다 (make): 만드는, 만듭니다, 만드세요
열다 (open): 여는, 엽니다, 여세요
팔다 (sell): 파는, 팝니다, 파세요

All follow the same rule:
ㄹ drops before ㄴ, ㅂ, ㅅ.

문을 여세요: please open the door.
문: door. 여세요: please open.

steps.title: 📋 ㄹ-Irregular Summary
steps.text:
RULE: ㄹ drops before ㄴ, ㅂ, ㅅ.

알다: 아는 / 압니다 / 아세요
살다: 사는 / 삽니다 / 사세요
만들다: 만드는 / 만듭니다 / 만드세요
열다: 여는 / 엽니다 / 여세요
팔다: 파는 / 팝니다 / 파세요
놀다: 노는 / 놉니다 / 노세요

ㄹ STAYS before:
-고: 알고, 살고, 만들고
-면: 알면, 살면, 만들면
-았/었어요: 알았어요, 살았어요
-(으)ㄹ 거예요: 알 거예요, 살 거예요

Mnemonic: ㄹ is shy around ㄴ, ㅂ, ㅅ.
(These three consonants make ㄹ hide.)

steps.en: 으-irregular: ㅡ drops before vowel endings
steps.exampleEn: to write → writes, to be big → is big
steps.note:
THE RULE: When a ㅡ-stem meets a VOWEL ending (-아/어), the ㅡ drops.

쓰다 (write): 쓰 + 어요 → ㅡ drops → 써요
크다 (big): 크 + 어요 → ㅡ drops → 커요
바쁘다 (busy): 바쁘 + 아요 → ㅡ drops → 바빠요
예쁘다 (pretty): 예쁘 + 어요 → ㅡ drops → 예뻐요

The vowel harmony uses the PREVIOUS syllable:
바쁘: previous syllable 바 has ㅏ (bright) → 아요 → 바빠요
예쁘: previous syllable 예 has ㅖ (dark) → 어요 → 예뻐요

steps.exampleEn: I'm so busy these days.
steps.note:
바쁘다: 으-irregular.

바쁘 + 아요 → ㅡ drops → 바빠요 (present)
바쁘 + 았어요 → ㅡ drops → 바빴어요 (past)

Why 아 not 어? Look at PREVIOUS syllable:
바 has ㅏ → bright → -아 ending → 바빠요.

요즘: these days/lately.
너무: too much/very.
요즘 너무 바빠요 = I'm so busy these days.

steps.exampleEn: The flower is pretty.
steps.note:
예쁘다: 으-irregular.

예쁘 + 어요 → ㅡ drops → 예뻐요 (present)
예쁘 + 었어요 → ㅡ drops → 예뻤어요 (past)

Why 어 not 아? Previous syllable:
예 has ㅖ → dark → -어 ending → 예뻐요.

steps.hint: ㄹ drops before ㅂ. 알 → 아 + ㅂ니다 = 압니다.
ㄹ drops before ㄴ. 살 → 사 + 는 = 사는.
ㅡ drops. Previous syllable 바 = bright → 아. 바빠요.
ㅡ drops. Previous syllable 예 = dark → 어. 예뻐요.
ㄹ is shy around ㄴ, ㅂ, ㅅ. It hides.
ㅡ drops. 크 is one syllable only → default 어. 커 + ㅆ어요 = 컸어요.

steps.s: 서울에 ___사람이 많아요. (Many people living in Seoul.)
steps.hint: 살다: ㄹ drops before ㄴ. 사 + 는 = 사는.

steps.s: 요즘 너무 ___. (I'm so busy these days.)
steps.hint: 바쁘다: ㅡ drops. 바 = bright → 아. 바빠요.

steps.s: 문을 ___. (Please open the door.)
steps.hint: 열다: ㄹ drops before ㅅ. 열 → 여 + 세요 = 여세요.

steps.title: Third Person Want — The Trap
steps.desc:
You know -고 싶어요: 'I want to.'

But what about 'HE wants to'?

In Korean, you can't say what someone ELSE wants using -고 싶어요.
You need -고 싶어하다.

This is one of the trickiest grammar points for foreigners.
goals: Understand why -고 싶다 is 1st person only
See the 하다 suffix for external observation
Preview for full treatment in Unit 15

steps.exampleEn: I want to go to Korea.
steps.note:
-고 싶다: I want to.

You learned this in Unit 4.
먹고 싶어요: I want to eat.
가고 싶어요: I want to go.

Why is this 1ST PERSON ONLY?
Because -고 싶다 describes an INTERNAL feeling.
You can know YOUR feelings, but you can't directly state someone else's.

저는 가고 싶어요. ✓ (I know my desire)
그 사람은 가고 싶어요. ✗ (How do you know their desire?)

steps.exampleEn: That person wants to go to Korea.
steps.note:
-고 싶어하다: he/she wants to.

싶어하다: shows external OBSERVATION of someone's desire.
하다 suffix = 'shows/acts like' (external behavior).

1st person: 가고 싶어요 (I feel I want to go)
3rd person: 가고 싶어해요 (I observe they want to go)

그 사람은 한국에 가고 싶어해요.
= That person wants to go to Korea.
(I can see/know this from their behavior.)

steps.title: 📋 The Internal/External Rule
steps.text:
Korean distinguishes INTERNAL vs EXTERNAL feelings:

INTERNAL (1st person) — I feel it:
가고 싶어요 (I want to go)
아파요 (I hurt)
좋아요 (I like it/it's good)

EXTERNAL (3rd person) — I observe it:
가고 싶어해요 (they want to go)
아파해요 (they seem to be in pain)
좋아해요 (they like it)

The -하다 suffix shifts from FEELING to OBSERVING.

⚡ JRTF: Full exploration in Unit 15.
This is a deep topic — for now, just know the 3rd person rule.
When you talk about someone ELSE's desires, add -하다.

저는 커피를 좋아해요 is an exception:
좋아하다 is ALREADY the externalized form.
For 1st person feelings: 커피가 좋아요.

steps.hint: 1st person feelings → -고 싶어요 (internal).
3rd person → -고 싶어하다. 먹고 싶어해요.

steps.q: Why can't you say 그 사람은 가고 싶어요?
opts: You can't directly state someone else's internal feelings
steps.ans: You can't directly state someone else's internal feelings
steps.hint: Korean: internal feelings = 1st person only. 3rd person needs -하다.

opts: It shifts from feeling to external observation
steps.ans: It shifts from feeling to external observation
steps.hint: -하다 = external action/behavior. You're observing, not feeling.

steps.q: This lesson is a ___ for Unit 15:
steps.hint: ⚡ JRTF: Full treatment in Unit 15 Lesson 7.

steps.s: 저는 한국에 가고 ___. (I want to go to Korea.)
steps.hint: 1st person → -고 싶어요.

steps.s: 그 사람은 한국에 가고 ___. (That person wants to go to Korea.)
steps.hint: 3rd person → -고 싶어해요.

steps.s: 동생이 아이스크림을 먹고 ___. (My younger sibling wants to eat ice cream.)
steps.hint: 3rd person (동생) → -고 싶어해요.

steps.s: 저는 커피를 마시고 {1}. 친구는 주스를 마시고 {2}.
steps.hint: I want to drink coffee. My friend wants to drink juice.

steps.q: Korean has the internal/external distinction because:
opts: Korean culture values not assuming others' feelings
steps.ans: Korean culture values not assuming others' feelings
steps.hint: Korean linguistic culture: you can speak your own feelings, but observe others' externally.

steps.q: 엄마가 한국 음식을 먹고 ___. (Mom wants to eat Korean food.)
steps.hint: 엄마 = 3rd person → -고 싶어해요.

steps.s: 저는 여행하고 ___. 친구도 여행하고 ___. (I want to travel. My friend also wants to travel.)
steps.hint: First blank: 1st person → 싶어요. Second blank... 3rd person → 싶어해요.

steps.s: 저는 커피를 마시고 {1}. 동생은 주스를 마시고 {2}. 엄마는 차를 마시고 {3}.
steps.hint: I want to drink coffee. My sibling wants juice. Mom wants tea. (1st → 싶어요, 3rd → 싶어해요)

steps.title: Unit 9 Complete — Future Tense & Speech Levels
steps.desc:
You've unlocked:

• Future tense (-(으)ㄹ 거예요)
• -아/어서 cause and sequence (with imperative restriction)
• ★ Three speech levels (해요체/합쇼체/반말)
• Plans vocabulary and dialogue
• -고 있다 and -고 나서
• ★ ㄹ and 으 irregular families
• -고 싶어하다 (3rd person want)

Almost done with A2!

steps.hint: Vowel stem + ㄹ 거예요. 갈 거예요.
Consonant stem + 습니다. 먹습니다.
ㄹ drops before ㄴ. 살 → 사는.
ㅡ drops. Previous syllable ㅏ = bright → 아. 바빠요.
3rd person → -고 싶어해요.

steps.q: 배가 고파서 밥 먹자! — This is:
opts: Wrong: -아/어서 can't be followed by suggestions
steps.ans: Wrong: -아/어서 can't be followed by suggestions
steps.hint: -아/어서 + imperative/suggestion = WRONG.
-고 있다 = progressive (is doing right now).

opts: Close friends your age or younger
steps.ans: Close friends your age or younger
steps.hint: 반말 only with close friends same age/younger. Never strangers/elders.
-거든요 = the reason is / you see.

steps.s: 내일 영화를 ___ 거예요. (I'm going to watch a movie tomorrow.)
steps.hint: 보다 → 볼 거예요.

steps.s: 가다 → 합쇼체: ___니다.
steps.hint: 가: vowel stem + ㅂ니다 = 갑니다.

steps.s: 밥을 먹___ 나서 커피를 마셨어요. (After eating, I drank coffee.)
steps.hint: -고 나서 = after doing.

steps.s: 열다 → 여세요 (please open). ㄹ drops before:
steps.hint: ㄹ drops before ㄴ, ㅂ, ㅅ. 열 + 세요 → 여세요.

steps.s: 내일 친구를 {1} 거예요. 같이 영화를 {2} 거예요. 밥을 먹{3} 나서 카페에 {4} 거예요.
steps.hint: Tomorrow I'll meet a friend. We'll watch a movie. After eating we'll go to a café.

steps.title: 🎉 Unit 9 Complete
steps.text:
What you can do now:

✅ Talk about the future: -(으)ㄹ 거예요
✅ Use all three speech levels (해요체/합쇼체/반말)
✅ Make, accept, and decline plans
✅ Handle ㄹ and 으 irregular verbs
✅ Use -아/어서 correctly (with imperative restriction)
✅ Express progressive actions (-고 있다)
✅ Sequence with -고 나서 (after doing)

One more unit to go for A2!

Unit 10: 한국 생활
Daily routine, home vocabulary, 반말 production,
편의점 and 배달 culture.

거의 다 왔어요! (You're almost there!)

steps.q: The Train now has ___ tense cars + other grammar cars:
steps.hint: Past: 았/었/했. Future: -(으)ㄹ 거. Plus conditional, ability, etc.
